title: The Everlasting Gospel
content:
آ آ آ آ The vision of Christ that thou dost see
آ آ آ آ Is my visionï؟½s greatest enemy.
آ آ آ آ Thine has a great hook nose like thine;
آ آ آ آ Mine has a snub nose like to mine.
آ آ آ آ Thine is the Friend of all Mankind;
آ آ آ آ Mine speaks in parables to the blind.
آ آ آ آ Thine loves the same world that mine hates;
آ آ آ آ Thy heaven doors are my hell gates.
آ آ آ آ Socrates taught what Meletus
آ آ آ آ Loathï؟½d as a nationï؟½s bitterest curse,
آ آ آ آ And Caiaphas was in his own mind
آ آ آ آ A benefactor to mankind.
آ آ آ آ Both read the Bible day and night,
آ آ آ آ But thou readï؟½st black where I read white.

آ آ آ آ Was Jesus gentle, or did He
آ آ آ آ Give any marks of gentility?
آ آ آ آ When twelve years old He ran away,
آ آ آ آ And left His parents in dismay.
آ آ آ آ When after three daysï؟½ sorrow found,
آ آ آ آ Loud as Sinaiï؟½s trumpet-sound:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½No earthly parents I confessï؟½
آ آ آ آ My Heavenly Fatherï؟½s business!
آ آ آ آ Ye understand not what I say,
آ آ آ آ And, angry, force Me to obey.
آ آ آ آ Obedience is a duty then,
آ آ آ آ And favour gains with God and men.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ John from the wilderness loud cried;
آ آ آ آ Satan gloried in his pride.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Come,ï؟½ said Satan, ï؟½come away,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll soon see if youï؟½ll obey!
آ آ آ آ John for disobedience bled,
آ آ آ آ But you can turn the stones to bread.
آ آ آ آ Godï؟½s high king and Godï؟½s high priest
آ آ آ آ Shall plant their glories in your breast,
آ آ آ آ If Caiaphas you will obey,
آ آ آ آ If Herod you with bloody prey
آ آ آ آ Feed with the sacrifice, and be
آ آ آ آ Obedient, fall down, worship me.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Thunders and lightnings broke around,
آ آ آ آ And Jesusï؟½ voice in thundersï؟½ sound:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Thus I seize the spiritual prey.
آ آ آ آ Ye smiters with disease, make way.
آ آ آ آ I come your King and God to seize,
آ آ آ آ Is God a smiter with disease?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ The God of this world ragï؟½d in vain:
آ آ آ آ He bound old Satan in His chain,
آ آ آ آ And, bursting forth, His furious ire
آ آ آ آ Became a chariot of fire.
آ آ آ آ Throughout the land He took His course,
آ آ آ آ And tracï؟½d diseases to their source.
آ آ آ آ He cursï؟½d the Scribe and Pharisee,
آ آ آ آ Trampling down hypocrisy.
آ آ آ آ Whereï؟½er His chariot took its way,
آ آ آ آ There Gates of Death let in the Day,
آ آ آ آ Broke down from every chain and bar;
آ آ آ آ And Satan in His spiritual war
آ آ آ آ Draggï؟½d at His chariot-wheels: loud howlï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The God of this world: louder rollï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The chariot-wheels, and louder still
آ آ آ آ His voice was heard from Zionï؟½s Hill,
آ آ آ آ And in His hand the scourge shone bright;
آ آ آ آ He scourgï؟½d the merchant Canaanite
آ آ آ آ From out the Temple of His Mind,
آ آ آ آ And in his body tight does bind
آ آ آ آ Satan and all his hellish crew;
آ آ آ آ And thus with wrath He did subdue
آ آ آ آ The serpent bulk of Natureï؟½s dross,
آ آ آ آ Till He had nailï؟½d it to the Cross.
آ آ آ آ He took on sin in the Virginï؟½s womb
آ آ آ آ And put it off on the Cross and tomb
آ آ آ آ To be worshippï؟½d by the Church of Rome.

آ آ آ آ Was Jesus humble? or did He
آ آ آ آ Give any proofs of humility?
آ آ آ آ Boast of high things with humble tone,
آ آ آ آ And give with charity a stone?
آ آ آ آ When but a child He ran away,
آ آ آ آ And left His parents in dismay.
آ آ آ آ When they had wanderï؟½d three days long
آ آ آ آ These were the words upon His tongue:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½No earthly parents I confess:
آ آ آ آ I am doing My Fatherï؟½s business.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ When the rich learnï؟½d Pharisee
آ آ آ آ Came to consult Him secretly,
آ آ آ آ Upon his heart with iron pen
آ آ آ آ He wrote ï؟½Ye must be born again.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ He was too proud to take a bribe;
آ آ آ آ He spoke with authority, not like a Scribe.
آ آ آ آ He says with most consummate art
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Follow Me, I am meek and lowly of heart,
آ آ آ آ As that is the only way to escape
آ آ آ آ The miserï؟½s net and the gluttonï؟½s trap.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ What can be done with such desperate fools
آ آ آ آ Who follow after the heathen schools?
آ آ آ آ I was standing by when Jesus died;
آ آ آ آ What I callï؟½d humility, they callï؟½d pride.
آ آ آ آ He who loves his enemies betrays his friends.
آ آ آ آ This surely is not what Jesus intends;
آ آ آ آ But the sneaking pride of heroic schools,
آ آ آ آ And the Scribesï؟½ and Phariseesï؟½ virtuous rules;
آ آ آ آ For He acts with honest, triumphant pride,
آ آ آ آ And this is the cause that Jesus dies.
آ آ آ آ He did not die with Christian ease,
آ آ آ آ Asking pardon of His enemies:
آ آ آ آ If He had, Caiaphas would forgive;
آ آ آ آ Sneaking submission can always live.
آ آ آ آ He had only to say that God was the Devil,
آ آ آ آ And the Devil was God, like a Christian civil;
آ آ آ آ Mild Christian regrets to the Devil confess
آ آ آ آ For affronting him thrice in the wilderness;
آ آ آ آ He had soon been bloody Caesarï؟½s elf,
آ آ آ آ And at last he would have been Caesar himself,
آ آ آ آ Like Dr. Priestly and Bacon and Newtonï؟½
آ آ آ آ Poor spiritual knowledge is not worth a button
آ آ آ آ For thus the Gospel Sir Isaac confutes:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½God can only be known by His attributes;
آ آ آ آ And as for the indwelling of the Holy Ghost,
آ آ آ آ Or of Christ and His Father, itï؟½s all a boast
آ آ آ آ And pride, and vanity of the imagination,
آ آ آ آ That disdains to follow this worldï؟½s fashion.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ To teach doubt and experiment
آ آ آ آ Certainly was not what Christ meant.
آ آ آ آ What was He doing all that time,
آ آ آ آ From twelve years old to manly prime?
آ آ آ آ Was He then idle, or the less
آ آ آ آ About His Fatherï؟½s business?
آ آ آ آ Or was His wisdom held in scorn
آ آ آ آ Before His wrath began to burn
آ آ آ آ In miracles throughout the land,
آ آ آ آ That quite unnervï؟½d the Seraph band?
آ آ آ آ If He had been Antichrist, Creeping Jesus,
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½d have done anything to please us;
آ آ آ آ Gone sneaking into synagogues,
آ آ آ آ And not usï؟½d the Elders and Priests like dogs;
آ آ آ آ But humble as a lamb or ass
آ آ آ آ Obeyï؟½d Himself to Caiaphas.
آ آ آ آ God wants not man to humble himself:
آ آ آ آ That is the trick of the Ancient Elf.
آ آ آ آ This is the race that Jesus ran:
آ آ آ آ Humble to God, haughty to man,
آ آ آ آ Cursing the Rulers before the people
آ آ آ آ Even to the Templeï؟½s highest steeple,
آ آ آ آ And when He humbled Himself to God
آ آ آ آ Then descended the cruel rod.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½If Thou Humblest Thyself, Thou humblest Me.
آ آ آ آ Thou also dwellï؟½st in Eternity.
آ آ آ آ Thou art a Man: God is no more:
آ آ آ آ Thy own Humanity learn to adore,
آ آ آ آ For that is My spirit of life.
آ آ آ آ Awake, arise to spiritual strife,
آ آ آ آ And Thy revenge abroad display
آ آ آ آ In terrors at the last Judgement Day.
آ آ آ آ Godï؟½s mercy and long suffering
آ آ آ آ Is but the sinner to judgement to bring.
آ آ آ آ Thou on the Cross for them shalt prayï؟½
آ آ آ آ And take revenge at the Last Day.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Jesus replied, and thunders hurlï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½I never will pray for the world.
آ آ آ آ Once I did so when I prayï؟½d in the Garden;
آ آ آ آ I wishï؟½d to take with Me a bodily pardon.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Can that which was of woman born,
آ آ آ آ In the absence of the morn,
آ آ آ آ When the Soul fell into sleep,
آ آ آ آ And Archangels round it weep,
آ آ آ آ Shooting out against the light
آ آ آ آ Fibres of a deadly night,
آ آ آ آ Reasoning upon its own dark fiction,
آ آ آ آ In doubt which is self-contradiction?
آ آ آ آ Humility is only doubt,
آ آ آ آ And does the sun and moon blot out,
آ آ آ آ Rooting over with thorns and stems
آ آ آ آ The buried soul and all its gems.
آ آ آ آ This lifeï؟½s five windows of the soul
آ آ آ آ Distorts the Heavens from pole to pole,
آ آ آ آ And leads you to believe a lie
آ آ آ آ When you see with, not throï؟½, the eye
آ آ آ آ That was born in a night, to perish in a night,
آ آ آ آ When the soul slept in the beams of light.

آ آ آ آ Did Jesus teach doubt? or did He
آ آ آ آ Give any lessons of philosophy,
آ آ آ آ Charge Visionaries with deceiving,
آ آ آ آ Or call men wise for not believing?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Was Jesus born of a Virgin pure
آ آ آ آ With narrow soul and looks demure?
آ آ آ آ If He intended to take on sin
آ آ آ آ The Mother should an harlot been,
آ آ آ آ Just such a one as Magdalen,
آ آ آ آ With seven devils in her pen.
آ آ آ آ Or were Jew virgins still more cursï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And more sucking devils nursï؟½d?
آ آ آ آ Or what was it which He took on
آ آ آ آ That He might bring salvation?
آ آ آ آ A body subject to be tempted,
آ آ آ آ From neither pain nor grief exempted;
آ آ آ آ Or such a body as might not feel
آ آ آ آ The passions that with sinners deal?
آ آ آ آ Yes, but they say He never fell.
آ آ آ آ Ask Caiaphas; for he can tell.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½He mockï؟½d the Sabbath, and He mockï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The Sabbathï؟½s God, and He unlockï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The evil spirits from their shrines,
آ آ آ آ And turnï؟½d fishermen to divines;
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½erturnï؟½d the tent of secret sins,
آ آ آ آ And its golden cords and pins,
آ آ آ آ In the bloody shrine of war
آ آ آ آ Pourï؟½d around from star to star,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Halls of justice, hating vice,
آ آ آ آ Where the Devil combs his lice.
آ آ آ آ He turnï؟½d the devils into swine
آ آ آ آ That He might tempt the Jews to dine;
آ آ آ آ Since which, a pig has got a look
آ آ آ آ That for a Jew may be mistook.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Obey your parents.ï؟½ï؟½What says He?
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Woman, what have I to do with thee?
آ آ آ آ No earthly parents I confess:
آ آ آ آ I am doing my Fatherï؟½s business.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ He scornï؟½d Earthï؟½s parents, scornï؟½d Earthï؟½s God,
آ آ آ آ And mockï؟½d the one and the otherï؟½s rod;
آ آ آ آ His seventy Disciples sent
آ آ آ آ Against Religion and Governmentï؟½
آ آ آ آ They by the sword of Justice fell,
آ آ آ آ And Him their cruel murderer tell.
آ آ آ آ He left His fatherï؟½s trade to roam,
آ آ آ آ A wandï؟½ring vagrant without home;
آ آ آ آ And thus He othersï؟½ labour stole,
آ آ آ آ That He might live above control.
آ آ آ آ The publicans and harlots He
آ آ آ آ Selected for His company,
آ آ آ آ And from the adulteress turnï؟½d away
آ آ آ آ Godï؟½s righteous law, that lost its prey.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Was Jesus chaste? or did He
آ آ آ آ Give any lessons of chastity?
آ آ آ آ The Morning blushï؟½d fiery red:
آ آ آ آ Mary was found in adulterous bed;
آ آ آ آ Earth groanï؟½d beneath, and Heaven above
آ آ آ آ Trembled at discovery of Love.
آ آ آ آ Jesus was sitting in Mosesï؟½ chair.
آ آ آ آ They brought the trembling woman there.
آ آ آ آ Moses commands she be stonï؟½d to death.
آ آ آ آ What was the sound of Jesusï؟½ breath?
آ آ آ آ He laid His hand on Mosesï؟½ law;
آ آ آ آ The ancient Heavens, in silent awe,
آ آ آ آ Writ with curses from pole to pole,
آ آ آ آ All away began to roll.
آ آ آ آ The Earth trembling and naked lay
آ آ آ آ In secret bed of mortal clay;
آ آ آ آ On Sinai felt the Hand Divine
آ آ آ آ Pulling back the bloody shrine;
آ آ آ آ And she heard the breath of God,
آ آ آ آ As she heard by Edenï؟½s flood:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Good and Evil are no more!
آ آ آ آ Sinaiï؟½s trumpets cease to roar!
آ آ آ آ Cease, finger of God, to write!
آ آ آ آ The Heavens are not clean in Thy sight.
آ آ آ آ Thou art good, and Thou alone;
آ آ آ آ Nor may the sinner cast one stone.
آ آ آ آ To be good only, is to be
آ آ آ آ A God or else a Pharisee.
آ آ آ آ Thou Angel of the Presence Divine,
آ آ آ آ That didst create this Body of Mine,
آ آ آ آ Wherefore hast thou writ these laws
آ آ آ آ And created Hellï؟½s dark jaws?
آ آ آ آ My Presence I will take from thee:
آ آ آ آ A cold leper thou shalt be.
آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ thou wast so pure and bright
آ آ آ آ That Heaven was impure in thy sight,
آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ thy oath turnï؟½d Heaven pale,
آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ thy covenant built Hellï؟½s jail,
آ آ آ آ Thoï؟½ thou didst all to chaos roll
آ آ آ آ With the Serpent for its soul,
آ آ آ آ Still the breath Divine does move,
آ آ آ آ And the breath Divine is Love.
آ آ آ آ Mary, fear not! Let me see
آ آ آ آ The seven devils that torment thee.
آ آ آ آ Hide not from My sight thy sin,
آ آ آ آ That forgiveness thou mayï؟½st win.
آ آ آ آ Has no man condemnï؟½d thee?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½No man, Lord.ï؟½ ï؟½Then what is he
آ آ آ آ Who shall accuse thee? Come ye forth,
آ آ آ آ Fallen fiends of heavenly birth,
آ آ آ آ That have forgot your ancient love,
آ آ آ آ And driven away my trembling Dove.
آ آ آ آ You shall bow before her feet;
آ آ آ آ You shall lick the dust for meat;
آ آ آ آ And thoï؟½ you cannot love, but hate,
آ آ آ آ Shall be beggars at Loveï؟½s gate.
آ آ آ آ What was thy love? Let Me see it;
آ آ آ آ Was it love or dark deceit?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Love too long from me has fled;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas dark deceit, to earn my bread;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas covet, or ï؟½twas custom, or
آ آ آ آ Some trifle not worth caring for;
آ آ آ آ That they may call a shame and sin
آ آ آ آ Loveï؟½s temple that God dwelleth in,
آ آ آ آ And hide in secret hidden shrine
آ آ آ آ The naked Human Form Divine,
آ آ آ آ And render that a lawless thing
آ آ آ آ On which the Soul expands its wing.
آ آ آ آ But this, O Lord, this was my sin,
آ آ آ آ When first I let these devils in,
آ آ آ آ In dark pretence to chastity
آ آ آ آ Blaspheming Love, blaspheming Thee,
آ آ آ آ Thence rose secret adulteries,
آ آ آ آ And thence did covet also rise.
آ آ آ آ My sin Thou hast forgiven me;
آ آ آ آ Canst Thou forgive my blasphemy?
آ آ آ آ Canst Thou return to this dark hell,
آ آ آ آ And in my burning bosom dwell?
آ آ آ آ And canst Thou die that I may live?
آ آ آ آ And canst Thou pity and forgive?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Then rollï؟½d the shadowy Man away
آ آ آ آ From the limbs of Jesus, to make them His prey,
آ آ آ آ An ever devouring appetite,
آ آ آ آ Glittering with festering venoms bright;
آ آ آ آ Crying ï؟½Crucify this cause of distress,
آ آ آ آ Who donï؟½t keep the secrets of holiness!
آ آ آ آ The mental powers by diseases we bind;
آ آ آ آ But He heals the deaf, the dumb, and the blind.
آ آ آ آ Whom God has afflicted for secret ends,
آ آ آ آ He comforts and heals and calls them friends.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ But, when Jesus was crucified,
آ آ آ آ Then was perfected His galling pride.
آ آ آ آ In three nights He devourï؟½d His prey,
آ آ آ آ And still He devours the body of clay;
آ آ آ آ For dust and clay is the Serpentï؟½s meat,
آ آ آ آ Which never was made for Man to eat.

آ آ آ آ Seeing this False Christ, in fury and passion
آ آ آ آ I made my voice heard all over the nation.
آ آ آ آ What are thoseï؟½

آ آ آ آ I am sure this Jesus will not do,
آ آ آ آ Either for Englishman or Jew.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, William Blake delivers a powerful performance in "The Everlasting Gospel"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
